title: I keep coming back to warehouse software
summary: An essay on warehouse software, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: warehouse software when the infrastructure finally became visible
body: There was a version of warehouse software circulating in March 2020 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. That kind of obviousness is often manufactured by selecting the friendliest possible conditions. Standing inside the unnerving quiet of a masked grocery line, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

The topic stays worth returning to because it behaves badly inside simple ideological boxes. Warehouse software can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. If the conversation is serious, it has to keep both of those truths on the table. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. What matters to me is what the tool normalizes while everyone is busy applauding the convenience.

By 2020, the systems were doing double duty as tools and as social signals. People were reaching for them because they solved problems, but also because they implied something flattering about the user. That is where my caution kicked in. Once people start borrowing identity from a system, the claims around it tend to grow faster than the proof.

Over time I have learned to watch what disappears once a story starts getting repeated professionally. The omissions are usually familiar, labor, maintenance, repair, emotional burden, governance, failure handling, and who absorbs the inconvenience. Warehouse software looked different to me once I framed it that way. The elegant solution started to look less elegant and more like negotiated compromise wrapped in nice copy. That does not make the thing useless. A lot of criticism is useful first because it restores readability.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me read the archive as a record of the technologies and of my own changing eye. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting to me is the slower drift in what starts to feel normal, what starts to feel extractive, and what still seems worth wanting. Warehouse software sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from March 2020: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. These days I read the burden more closely than the forecast. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: The problem with fragile supply chains is usually the difference between resilience and improvisation
The problem with tiny labs is usually the way optimism can hide a labor model
